headline: ENERGY RALLY PAUSES — GRAINS READY FOR MONDAY TEST
subheadline: Crude's 7% surge cools but leaves input costs elevated as planting season intensifies.
lead: Friday's grain weakness — corn dropped to $4.52/bu and beans fell to $11.63/bu — sets up a critical test when markets reopen Monday morning. With crude oil holding near $112 despite energy's pause, farmers face elevated input costs just as planting pressure builds across the Upper Midwest.
teaser: Energy costs stabilize but grain weakness demands attention as planting season heats up.

one_number.context: Diesel and fertilizer costs remain elevated even after crude's rally paused Friday. This price level adds roughly $15-20/acre to operating costs compared to last spring's levels.

sections.title: WEEK AHEAD: GRAIN REBOUND WATCH
sections.body: Corn at $4.52/bu as of Friday's close represents key technical support after Thursday's energy-driven selloff. December corn futures at $4.81/bu show new-crop premiums intact, suggesting market confidence in demand fundamentals. Weather premium season officially begins in two weeks — any planting delays or early drought signals could spark quick rallies. Soybean weakness to $11.63/bu looks like profit-taking rather than demand destruction.
sections.bottom_line: Grains testing support levels — watch for technical buying on Monday morning.
sections.farmer_action: Hold current positions but watch Monday's open closely — buying interest below $4.50 corn would signal strong technical support.

sections.title: LIVESTOCK DIVERGENCE CONTINUES
sections.body: Live cattle at $246.20 as of Friday's close maintains near-record territory, reflecting tight supply fundamentals that aren't going away. Class III milk jumped to $18.14, up 1.2% Friday as spring flush expectations moderate. Hog volatility continues with Friday's decline to $104.45 following this week's explosive rally. Feed costs remain manageable despite energy pressures, keeping livestock margins attractive.
sections.bottom_line: Cattle strength continues, hogs cooling from recent explosion.
sections.farmer_action: Cattle producers should consider pricing protection on current positions — margins are historically strong but volatility is increasing.

sections.title: MONDAY WATCH LIST: INPUT COSTS
sections.body: WTI crude's hold above $112 keeps diesel and nitrogen costs elevated just as spring application and planting accelerate. Natural gas at $2.81 provides some relief for nitrogen producers, but distribution bottlenecks mean farm-gate prices lag commodity moves. Soybean oil's 1.6% Friday gain to $69.00 signals biodiesel demand remains robust despite renewable diesel competition.
sections.bottom_line: Energy costs stabilizing but remain elevated for spring operations.
sections.farmer_action: Lock remaining diesel needs now — current levels are workable but vulnerable to geopolitical spikes during driving season.

sections.title: MACRO SETUP: DOLLAR STEADY
sections.body: Dollar index holding exactly at 100.00 as of Friday's close provides neutral backdrop for agricultural exports. Gold's slight dip to $4,703 suggests inflation concerns moderating despite energy strength. S&P 500 at 6,582 shows broader market confidence, but agricultural sector rotation has stalled as investors await clearer planting progress signals.
sections.bottom_line: Stable macro environment but agricultural focus shifting to weather.
sections.farmer_action: Monitor weather forecasts closely — macro factors taking backseat to seasonal fundamentals for next six weeks.

the_more_you_know.title: Why Planting Dates Matter More Than Markets Think
the_more_you_know.body: Every day corn planting is delayed past the optimal April 15-May 15 window costs roughly 1 bushel per acre in yield potential. Most traders focus on acres planted, but timing quality matters just as much. A wet spring that delays planting by two weeks can cost 150 million bushels nationally even if total acres remain unchanged. This is why weather premiums build so quickly once we hit mid-April.

watch_list.desc: Grain futures open — watch for technical buying if corn holds above $4.50
Weather models for next 10 days — planting window opening across Iowa/Illinois
USDA Export Sales report — monitor corn and soy demand signals
Weekly ethanol production data — corn demand indicator
Crop Progress report — first official planting pace update of season

daily_quote.text: The farmer is the only man in our economy who buys everything at retail, sells everything at wholesale, and pays the freight both ways.

source_summary: CBOT/CME futures (Friday close), NYMEX energy, currency markets
